records.ety: [OE. vanissen, OF. vanir (in comp.): cf. OF. envanir, esvanir, esvanur, F. s'vanouir; fr. L. vanus empty, vain; cf. L. vanescere, evanescere, to vanish. See Vain, and cf. Evanescent,-ish.]
records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Vanished (vn"sht); p. pr. & vb. n. Vanishing.]

defs: To pass from a visible to an invisible state; to go out of sight; to disappear; to fade; as, vapor vanishes from the sight by being dissipated; a ship vanishes from the sight of spectators on land.
quotes: The horse vanished . . . out of sight.
The champions vanished from their posts with the speed of lightning.
Gliding from the twilight past to vanish among realities.

defs: To be annihilated or lost; to pass away.

The brief terminal part of a vowel or vocal element, differing more or less in quality from the main part; as, a as in ale ordinarily ends with a vanish of i as in ill, o as in old with a vanish of oo as in foot.
notes: ☞ The vanish is included by Mr. Bell under the general term glide.
